title: The problems with plant based meat

chunks.title: Intuitive Eating Explained
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses the concept of intuitive eating, emphasizing that it involves listening to one's body signals regarding hunger and food choices. They highlight that it becomes challenging for individuals who are not healthy and miss the signals due to unhealthy eating patterns.
chunks.transcript: it's intuitive eating that's something that's more kind of like scientism type demographic of people be like oh you need intuitively oh God I'm there's a lot of things that I I've had a lot of resistance around over the years and now I'm kind of was like yeah I Didn't Do It Like that's kind of where I'm at yeah sorry guys you know like I I know when I've eaten too much yeah I know when I drank too much water I know when I need some water you know there's there's I know when if I eat some whatever some organs or some steak or some whatever thing is how that makes you feel I know how I feel off I eat a cookie like I I that that's intuitive eating it doesn't need to be some Airy fairy weird things just like it's just listening to your body it's not that weird I think it breaks down I think it breaks down for people when they are Baseline not healthy right you know if somebody is obese and metabolically unwell uh I I'm intuitively eating a pizza right right okay that doesn't really work but if at level you're at with the intentional amount of food and I'm the same way I can tell when I've eaten something that doesn't clean up the signal to be able to actually hear signal the noise the message the noise yeah you can differentiate I'm just filled with noise versus there's actually some signal here yeah and I've cleared it enough maybe not perfect but enough that I can hear the signal yeah and I can I can feel when there's the feedback of the noise coming in it's like oh I don't know I don't like that but if you're just all cacophonous noise good luck with intuitive eating yeah yeah that's the truth that's the challenge with it yeah

chunks.title: Critique of Fake Meat Products
chunks.summary: The conversation shifts to the nature of fake meat products and their resemblance to real meat. The speakers argue that these products reflect a craving for meat among vegans and highlight the irony of creating meat substitutes that are often processed and contain numerous unnatural ingredients.

chunks.title: Plant-Based Milks and Their Misleading Benefits
chunks.summary: The speakers criticize plant-based milks, claiming that the health benefits marketed are misleading. They argue that these products typically involve harmful ingredients and farming practices, pointing out that they lack the essential nutrients found in animal products.
chunks.transcript: because I I love on plant-based milks because the rhetoric around plant-based milks is so brazenly false that it gets me really triggered and so you look at Oatley or you look at whatever oat milk you've got we pulled two of them off the shelf at Central Market today and both of them say this sort of boilerplate rhetoric better for you better for the planet and you think oh it's better for the planet that you're mono cropping a field full of pesticides that you're destroying the complete ecosystem in that field everything that was there before that you're tilling the soil that you're depleting the soil of nutrients and that you're putting all sorts of harmful things into this land that's that's better for the planet potentially leading to desertification exactly that's better for the planet but that's what Oatley says on the side of their bottle and they also say better for you and I think well better for you when it contains seed oils like no better for you because it's oxidizing your LDL better for you because it's increasing your cardiovascular disease risk no it's we've just gone so astray like people just don't understand the reality and there's no you can say anything you want I guess in these packages and that's you know that's why I feel compelled to say hey I think this is false I think this is a lie on the side of these bottles this plant-based milk is not better for

chunks.title: Nutritional Value of Animal vs. Plant-Based Foods
chunks.summary: The discussion highlights the nutritional superiority of animal-based foods compared to plant-based options. The speakers note that animal foods provide a variety of essential nutrients naturally, whereas plant-based alternatives often require synthetic additives to enhance their nutritional profiles.
chunks.transcript: it's almost like a shotgun approach of getting vital nutrients yeah it's kind of an interesting analogy it's just popping up to my mind it's like it doesn't need anything else it just comes equipped with what you need it's kind of interesting that in those plant-based milk substitutes it needs a whole bunch of stuff to be okay and stabilizers and thickeners and sweeteners and all of that it's kind of interesting like it's almost like allegory it's very ironic if you look at the nutrition facts on a package of ground beef which is just beef there's a lot of nutrients in there there's iron there's B12 there's K2 and not all these are on the labels but there's bioavailable protein but if you there's creatine there's carnitine if you look at the nutrition facts on a bottle of plant milk that's just almonds and water there's like nothing on there yeah there's no there's no nutrients there and the nutrients that are there are not very bioavailable like say you'd say oh almonds are high in magnesium well that magnesium is not very bioavailable because it's chelated by phytic acid so in order for plant-based meat quote unquote to have anything of substance on the nutrition label they have to add a bunch of synthetic vitamins and this is the pro this is the thing with impossible burger or beyond meat if you look at their nutrition label it might say oh it's got this zinc or it's got this iron or it's got this well that's because they added synthetic things to it it's not actually in any of the food that they put in there there's not a lot of food in plant-based meat but one could argue that soybeans or methyl cellulose which is essentially wood dust that's not really food but you know to make it look like there's nutrients they have to add a bunch of synthetic nutrients if you look at any animal food whether it's eggs or meat or milk those
